reasoning_note: Hypoxia is commonly identified when dissolved oxygen concentration is lower than 0.06 mol/m3 (equivalent to 2 mg/L or 60 mmol/m3). When used to delineate a qualifying region, first take the unweighted mean of the criterion variable over depth and time, then apply the threshold to the resulting 2D field to obtain the spatial mask. But for hypoxic water VOLUME or thickness, do not average first: threshold per month and per depth layer, then dz-weight and sum over layers, cell area, and region — one value per month.

Heatwave months are identified when monthly sea surface temperature exceeds the calendar-month-specific 90th percentile derived from the baseline SST time series. The threshold is computed independently for each calendar month using raw SST values rather than anomalies.

An oxygen minimum zone is identified where dissolved oxygen concentration is lower than 0.06 mol/m3. When used to delineate a qualifying region, first take the unweighted mean of the criterion variable over depth and time, then apply the threshold to the resulting 2D field to obtain the spatial mask. But for OMZ VOLUME or thickness, do not average first: threshold per month and per depth layer, then dz-weight and sum over layers, cell area, and region — one value per month.

Unless otherwise specified, anomalies are calculated by subtracting the climatological mean of the corresponding calendar month to remove the seasonal cycle. The anomaly computation should be performed before any spatial averaging, temporal averaging, or other aggregation operations. If the question specifies a baseline period, use it; otherwise default to 1995–2014. Climate mode indices such as ENSO and PDO may use separate standardized definitions and baseline periods.

Climatology has two distinct usages that must be disambiguated by how the result is consumed. (1) Mean-state field (DEFAULT when the climatology is itself the analysis target, e.g. a regional or global mean state, or the per-model field whose inter-model spread, difference, or ratio is being evaluated): collapse the ENTIRE selected time period into a SINGLE field by taking the plain time mean over all time steps, with no calendar-month grouping; the result carries no month or season dimension. (2) Monthly or seasonal-cycle climatology (used when the seasonal cycle must be represented or removed, e.g. to form an anomaly as value minus climatology, to deseasonalize a series, or when a monthly/seasonal cycle is explicitly requested): when no season is specified, average all values within each calendar month independently to yield a 12-step monthly cycle; when a season (DJF/MAM/JJA/SON) is requested, first compute the seasonal mean for each individual year-season separately, then average these per-year seasonal means across all years. Reference period: if the question states an explicit time period, use that period; otherwise, and when not associated with special climate modes such as ENSO or PDO, use the 1995-2014 reference period. No sliding windows or temporal smoothing are applied in any case.

Oligotrophic conditions are primarily determined using chlorophyll concentration lower than 1e-7 kg/m3. Only if the chlorophyll data file does not exist, nitrate concentration lower than 1e-4 mol/m3 may be used as the first fallback indicator. Phosphate concentration lower than 1e-5 mol/m3 should only be used when both chlorophyll and nitrate data are unavailable. For determining whether a region is oligotrophic, the selected variable should first be averaged over the target depth range and time period. For comparing which of two periods is more oligotrophic, the selected variable should be averaged over all spatial, temporal, and vertical dimensions before comparison. When used to delineate a qualifying region, first take the unweighted mean of the criterion variable over depth and time, then apply the threshold to the resulting 2D field to obtain the spatial mask. But for the oligotrophic water VOLUME (or its anomaly/trend), do not average over depth or time first: threshold per month and per depth layer, dz-weight and sum the qualifying layers times cell area, then sum over the region — one volume per month.

Eutrophic conditions are primarily determined using chlorophyll concentration higher than 5e-6 kg/m3. If chlorophyll data are unavailable, nitrate concentration higher than 1e-2 mol/m3 may be used as the first fallback indicator. Phosphate concentration higher than 1e-3 mol/m3 should only be used when both chlorophyll and nitrate data are unavailable. For determining whether a region is eutrophic, the selected variable should first be averaged over the target depth range and time period. For comparing which of two periods is more eutrophic, the selected variable should be averaged over all spatial, temporal, and vertical dimensions before comparison.

El Nino months are identified when the 3-month running mean sea surface temperature anomaly in the Nino 3.4 region exceeds +0.5 degC relative to the 1971-2000 monthly climatology for at least 3 consecutive months. When this phase is combined with annual or decadal aggregation, the phase classification and month selection are applied first at monthly resolution; each year is then averaged using only the months in this phase, before any trend or correlation. If there are any special instructions regarding the reference time, please follow them first.

La Nina months are identified when the 3-month running mean sea surface temperature anomaly in the Nino 3.4 region is lower than -0.5 degC relative to the 1971-2000 monthly climatology for at least 3 consecutive months. When this phase is combined with annual or decadal aggregation, the phase classification and month selection are applied first at monthly resolution; each year is then averaged using only the months in this phase, before any trend or correlation. If there are any special instructions regarding the reference time, please follow them first.

Neutral ENSO months are identified when the 3-month running mean sea surface temperature anomaly in the Nino 3.4 region remains between -0.5 degC and +0.5 degC relative to the 1971-2000 monthly climatology, or when warm/cold anomalies do not persist for at least 3 consecutive months. When this phase is combined with annual or decadal aggregation, the phase classification and month selection are applied first at monthly resolution; each year is then averaged using only the months in this phase, before any trend or correlation. If there are any special instructions regarding the reference time, please follow them first.
Iron limitation is identified when the dissolved iron to nitrate ratio (dfe/no3) is lower than 5e-5, indicating insufficient iron availability relative to nitrogen.
Nitrogen limitation is identified when the nitrate to phosphate ratio (no3/po4) is lower than 16, following the Redfield stoichiometric ratio.
Phosphorus limitation is identified when the nitrate to phosphate ratio (no3/po4) is higher than 16, indicating phosphate deficiency relative to nitrogen.
Silicate limitation is identified when the silicate to nitrate ratio (si/no3) is lower than 1, indicating insufficient silicate availability relative to nitrogen.
Suboxia is identified when dissolved oxygen concentration is lower than 0.020 mol/m3.

Nitrogen fixation signature is identified using the N* tracer calculated as no3 - 16 × po4. Negative N* values indicate excess phosphorus relative to nitrate and are commonly associated with regions influenced by nitrogen fixation.

Denitrification signature is identified when the N* tracer calculated as no3 - 16 × po4 is lower than -2e-3 mol/m3 together with dissolved oxygen concentration lower than 0.020 mol/m3, indicating nitrate loss under oxygen-deficient conditions.
Ocean acidification stress is identified when seawater pH is lower than 7.9, indicating biologically stressful acidified conditions.
Strong current regions are identified when horizontal current speed calculated as sqrt(uo^2 + vo^2) exceeds 0.5 m/s.

PDO phases are classified directly from the sign of the standardized monthly PDO index. Positive PDO phase corresponds to index values greater than zero, negative PDO phase corresponds to index values smaller than zero, and neutral phase corresponds to zero. Non-positive months include both negative and neutral (index <= 0), and non-negative months include both positive and neutral (index >= 0). No magnitude threshold, running mean, or persistence filter is applied. Classification is performed directly on the original monthly-resolution PDO index.

The PDO index is computed from surface thetao anomalies using the standard EOF-based Mantua-style method. Monthly anomalies are calculated relative to the 1971-2000 monthly climatology, and the global mean anomaly is removed at each time step before EOF analysis. The PDO spatial domain is defined as 20-70 degrees north and 120 degrees east to 100 degrees west. Square-root cosine latitude weighting is applied before singular value decomposition, and the leading EOF mode is used to construct the standardized PDO index. The EOF sign is corrected so that positive PDO corresponds to positive SST anomalies in the eastern North Pacific (30-50 degrees north, 200-240 degrees east). Calculations are performed directly on the original monthly-resolution data.

Mixed layer depth is defined as the shallowest depth level where the density difference relative to the reference level (the depth level nearest to 10 m) exceeds 0.03 kg/m3. If two levels are equidistant from 10 m (e.g. 5 m and 15 m), use the deeper one, matching xarray sel(method='nearest'). If no depth level satisfies the criterion, the deepest available depth level is returned. When a quantity is requested 'in the average mixed layer depth', collapse this per-cell, per-month MLD field to a single scalar — the area-weighted spatial mean over the region followed by the unweighted temporal mean over the period.

Thermocline depth is computed as the depth corresponding to the maximum absolute vertical temperature gradient |dT/dz| within the 10–500 m depth range. Before gradient calculation, a centered 3-point rolling mean is applied along the depth dimension for smoothing. If fewer than 3 valid depth levels exist within the specified search range, the full vertical profile is used instead. When a quantity is requested 'in the average thermocline depth', collapse this per-cell, per-month thermocline-depth field to a single scalar — the area-weighted spatial mean over the region followed by the unweighted temporal mean over the period.

Water-column stability is quantified using the Brunt-Vaisala frequency squared (N²). The stability index is calculated as the depth-mean N² within the specified depth range. If no depth range is provided, use 0-200 m by default; otherwise follow the user-specified depth range.

By default, data are horizontally regridded onto a regular 1 degree latitude-longitude grid using bilinear interpolation. Rectilinear grids use xarray interp, while curvilinear grids use scipy griddata with linear interpolation after longitude normalization to [0,360). Periodic padding is applied near the dateline to maintain interpolation continuity across global domains. For comparisons between different datasets, vertical averaging should be applied before horizontal regridding unless otherwise specified by the user.

By default, significance refers to the statistical significance of a linear temporal trend unless otherwise specified by the user. Calculations are performed directly on the original monthly-resolution data without temporal smoothing, deseasonalization, or aggregation; otherwise specified by the user. Statistical significance is evaluated using a two-sided test with a significance level of 0.1; the same two-sided alpha = 0.1 also applies to correlation significance (its Pearson two-sided p-value < 0.1). When instead the significance being judged is that of inter-model spread (e.g. the spread/variability of a climatology across multiple models), spread is quantified by the coefficient of variation (CV), computed per grid cell as the across-model standard deviation (ddof=1) divided by the absolute value of the across-model mean, with cells whose absolute mean is below 1e-12 set to NaN to avoid unstable division. The per-cell CV field is then collapsed to a single domain-wide scalar via a cos(latitude)-weighted spatial mean, and the spread is deemed significant if this domain-average CV exceeds 0.5.

Which dimensions to average over must be judged from the user question itself. For example, in 'average dissolved organic carbon inventory' the inventory is already a sum over the selected region and depth range, so the average applies only to the time dimension. By default, temporal and vertical means are computed as simple arithmetic averages without weighting. Horizontal spatial means over latitude-longitude domains are computed using area weighting. Cell area should preferentially use the native areacello variable associated with the corresponding experiment and source dataset. Only when areacello is unavailable or cannot be loaded should grid cell area be approximated from latitude-longitude coordinates. For regular rectilinear grids, cell area is computed from latitude and longitude bounds using spherical geometry. For curvilinear grids, area is approximated using cos(latitude) multiplied by local latitude and longitude coordinate gradients.

By default, change is computed as the difference between two calculated quantities. For expressions using 'between A and B', the result is computed as B minus A, where A and B follow the order in which the two quantities (e.g. time periods) appear in the user's original question (B is the second-mentioned quantity, A is the first-mentioned). This textual/phrasing order governs the sign regardless of the chronological or temporal order of the quantities. For expressions using 'relative to', the result is computed as the target quantity minus the reference quantity. The calculation should always preserve the original physical units unless additional normalization is explicitly requested; the result is an absolute difference in those units, not a relative or percentage change.

Difference is computed as the first referenced quantity minus the second referenced quantity, where 'first' and 'second' follow the order in which the quantities appear in the user's original question (first-mentioned minus second-mentioned). This textual/phrasing order governs the sign regardless of the chronological or temporal order of the quantities. For comparisons such as 'qualified versus non-qualified' conditions, the qualified condition is treated as the first term unless explicitly stated otherwise. The resulting value preserves the original units of the analyzed variable; it is an absolute difference in those units, not a relative or percentage change.

By default, layer thickness is computed using the bounded method. Thickness is defined as the deepest qualifying depth minus the shallowest qualifying depth along the depth coordinate. Depth layer bounds are not used, and layer thickness is derived directly from differences between depth coordinate values. If the deepest valid depth level still satisfies the condition, the deepest qualifying depth is used as the lower bound.

By default, volume is computed as grid-cell area multiplied by vertical layer thickness. If the dataset contains explicit depth-bound variables such as lev_bnds, depth_bnds, olevel_bnds, or deptht_bounds, layer thickness is computed directly from these depth bounds. Only when no valid depth-bound variable is available should layer thickness be approximated using differences between adjacent depth coordinate values. Grid-cell area should preferentially use the native areacello variable associated with the corresponding dataset and experiment. If areacello is unavailable, area is approximated from latitude-longitude geometry. For regular rectilinear grids, cell area is computed from latitude and longitude bounds using spherical geometry. For curvilinear grids, area is approximated using cos(latitude) combined with local latitude and longitude coordinate gradients. Any statistic (mean, min, max, etc.) requested on a volume is taken over the time dimension only, since the volume is already summed over depth and the region, giving one value per month.

Annual mean values are computed directly from the original monthly-resolution data using calendar-year resampling with year-end labels. Temporal averaging is performed as an unweighted arithmetic mean over all available monthly values within each year. Years with incomplete monthly coverage are still retained and averaged using the available months only.

Annual trends are calculated by first resampling the original monthly-resolution data into annual means using year-end resampling and unweighted arithmetic averaging. Years with incomplete monthly coverage are retained using the available months only. By default, a linear trend is then estimated from the resulting annual time series unless otherwise specified by the user.

Decadal linear trends are computed by first resampling the original monthly-resolution data into annual means using year-end resampling and unweighted arithmetic averaging. Years with incomplete monthly coverage are retained using the available months only. A linear trend is estimated from the resulting annual time series, and the resulting slope is multiplied by 10 to express the rate of change per decade.

By default, lag refers to positive monthly offsets from 0 to 12 months. For a lag value k, the first time series is truncated to x[0:n-k] and the second to y[k:n] before correlation calculation. Pearson correlation is computed using only paired finite values. Lag strength is determined by the maximum absolute correlation coefficient across all tested lags. At least 24 valid paired samples are required after lag alignment, otherwise the lag correlation is treated as invalid. If either aligned series has zero variance, the correlation is returned as NaN. Before this lag scan, each monthly series is deseasonalized by subtracting its analysis-period calendar-month climatology, so the selected lag is not dominated by the shared seasonal cycle. This deseasonalization applies only to a monthly-lag scan over monthly-resolution series; it does not apply to a single zero-lag correlation or to series already aggregated to annual or coarser resolution.

By default, correlation refers to the Pearson linear correlation coefficient between the two value time series themselves (the paired values at each time step), not between their fitted trend slopes. The two series are first aligned using their shared time coordinates with inner joining. Correlation is computed only from paired finite values. At least 3 valid paired samples are required for calculation. If either valid series has zero variance, both the correlation coefficient and p-value are returned as NaN.

Two distinct cases must be handled differently. CASE 1 (concentration variables, unit mol m-3 or kg m-3: dissic, dissoc, o2, no3, po4, si, chl): the 'INVENTORY' is NOT already integrated and MUST be computed as grid-cell volume multiplied by the original concentration, then summed vertically over depth and over all selected grid cells. For example, dissolved inorganic carbon inventory is computed by volume-integrating dissic, and dissolved organic carbon inventory by volume-integrating dissoc.  Any statistic (mean, min, max, etc.) requested on an inventory is taken over the time dimension only, since its spatial and vertical dimensions are already summed away. CASE 2 (native vertically integrated stock variables, unit kg m-2: intdic, intdoc, intpoc): these 'CONTENT' fields are ALREADY depth-integrated, so they MUST be used directly and MUST NOT be re-integrated over depth or multiplied by layer thickness/volume; for a regional total, multiply by horizontal cell area and sum over grid cells only. Do not confuse the two: 'dissolved inorganic carbon INVENTORY' (CASE 1, from dissic) is not the same operation as 'dissolved inorganic carbon CONTENT'=intdic (CASE 2, used directly). In all cases, regional aggregation uses total summation, never spatial averaging. Total organic carbon content is the CASE 2 example computed directly as intdoc + intpoc (no extra depth integration), since particulate organic carbon has no concentration variable and is only available as the integrated intpoc.

term: dissolved inorganic carbon to alkalinity ratio
reasoning_note: When a ratio is treated as a derived variable, the ratio should be calculated first from the original variables before applying subsequent operations such as anomaly, climatology, trend, or averaging. For example, anomalies of nitrate to phosphate ratio should be computed from the ratio field itself. However, if the user explicitly requests the ratio between already aggregated statistics, such as the ratio of mean variable A to mean variable B, each variable should first be processed independently and the ratio applied only as the final step.

For inter-model or ensemble analyses, all calculations should first be performed independently for each model or data source using the original workflow. Cross-model aggregation is applied only after all per-model computations are completed. Ensemble statistics such as mean or standard deviation are then calculated along the model dimension with missing values ignored. When the per-model results still retain a spatial field, compute the cross-model statistic per grid cell (averaging only over models with valid data there) before any spatial average; do not reduce each model to a regional scalar first, since the two orderings differ whenever model coverage differs, not only for nonlinear statistics such as the standard deviation (ddof=1).

Spread is computed as the coefficient of variation across models at each grid cell. The numerator is the sample standard deviation across the model dimension, and the denominator is the absolute value of the multi-model mean. Grid cells with absolute mean smaller than 1e-12 are assigned NaN to avoid unstable division.
Refers to the uppermost ocean layer within 0-10 m depth. Judge membership by each vertical layer's center depth, i.e. a layer participates in full when its center depth lies within the range.
Refers to the deepest available ocean grid layer at each horizontal location.
Refers to the shallow upper ocean within 0-50 m depth. Judge membership by each vertical layer's center depth, i.e. a layer participates in full when its center depth lies within the range.
Refers to the upper ocean water column within 0-300 m depth. Judge membership by each vertical layer's center depth, i.e. a layer participates in full when its center depth lies within the range.

Refers to the ocean layer below the immediate surface ocean within 50-200 m depth. Judge membership by each vertical layer's center depth, i.e. a layer participates in full when its center depth lies within the range.
Refers to intermediate ocean waters within 300-1000 m depth. Judge membership by each vertical layer's center depth, i.e. a layer participates in full when its center depth lies within the range.
Refers to the sunlit upper ocean layer within 0-150 m depth. Judge membership by each vertical layer's center depth, i.e. a layer participates in full when its center depth lies within the range.

Refers to deep ocean waters below 1000 m depth extending to the seafloor. Judge membership by each vertical layer's center depth, i.e. a layer participates in full when its center depth lies within the range.
Refers to the Arctic Ocean north of 66N.
Refers to the tropical Pacific region within 20S-20N and 220E-280E.
Refers to the equatorial Pacific region within 10S-10N and 140E-280E.
Refers to the western Pacific warm pool region within 10S-10N and 130E-180E.
Refers to the South Pacific Convergence Zone within 25S-0 and 150E-240E.
Refers to the North Pacific subtropical gyre region within 10N-40N and 120E-260E.
Refers to the North Atlantic subtropical gyre region within 10N-40N and 280E-360E.
Refers to the North Atlantic Drift region within 40N-60N and 280E-360E.
Refers to the subpolar North Atlantic region within 45N-70N and 290E-360E.
Refers to the Agulhas Current region within 45S-20S and 10E-40E.
Refers to the Southern Ocean south of 40S.
Refers to the Indian Ocean basin within 30S-30N and 20E-120E.
Refers to the Indian Ocean Dipole analysis region within 20S-20N and 40E-110E.
Refers to the Bay of Bengal region within 5N-25N and 80E-100E.
Refers to the Arabian Sea region within 5N-25N and 50E-75E.
Refers to the Labrador Sea region within 50N-65N and 280E-320E.
Refers to the Gulf of Mexico region within 18N-31N and 260E-290E.
Refers to the Caribbean Sea region within 9N-23N and 270E-290E.
Refers to the Mediterranean Sea region within 30N-46N and 350E-40E.
Refers to the Sea of Japan region within 34N-52N and 127E-142E.
Refers to the South China Sea region within 3N-24N and 105E-121E.
Refers to the Coral Sea region within 25S-8S and 145E-165E.
Refers to the California Current System region within 20N-45N and 230E-245E.
Refers to the Canary Current System region within 15N-35N and 340E-360E.
Refers to the Benguela Upwelling System within 35S-10S and 5E-20E.
Refers to the Peru-Chile Current System within 45S-0 and 285E-300E.
Refers to the Kuroshio Extension region within 25N-40N and 140E-170E.

Niño regions correspond to predefined latitude-longitude bounding boxes in the equatorial Pacific used for ENSO diagnostics. Unless otherwise specified by the user, 'ENSO region' should default to the Niño 3.4 region.
Refers to the recent historical climate period spanning 1995-2014.
Refers to the late 21st century projection period spanning 2081-2100.
Refers to the pre-industrial reference period spanning 1850-1900.
Refers to the early 20th century period spanning 1901-1930.
Refers to the mid-20th century period spanning 1931-1960.
Refers to the late 20th century period spanning 1961-2000.
Refers to the early 21st century period spanning 2000-2014.
Refers to the modern climatological reference period spanning 1981-2010.
Refers to the late Little Ice Age period spanning 1850-1879.
Refers to the industrial onset period spanning 1880-1910.
Refers to the pre-modern warming reference period spanning 1960-1989.
Refers to the CMIP5 reference period spanning 1986-2005.
Refers to the WMO climatological normal period spanning 1991-2010.
Refers to the early historical baseline period spanning 1850-1950.
Refers to the early satellite-era baseline period spanning 1979-2000.
Refers to the full satellite-era historical period spanning 1979-2014.
Refers to the OISST standard climatology period spanning 1982-2011.
Refers to the mid-to-late 20th century baseline period spanning 1950-2000.
Refers to the late 20th century baseline period spanning 1961-2000.
Refers to the late CMIP6 historical period spanning 1985-2014.

Unless otherwise specified, seasons are interpreted using Northern Hemisphere conventions. Seasonal calculations require complete seasonal coverage within the selected time range; incomplete seasons should be discarded. For example, the range 1921-01 to 1921-10 only contains one complete season (JJA), while incomplete DJF, MAM, and SON periods are excluded. DJF spans two calendar years and is labeled using the year containing January and February following standard climate and oceanographic conventions. For example, December 2019 combined with January-February 2020 is labeled as DJF 2020. MAM, JJA, and SON remain within a single calendar year.
Nitrate star is calculated as nitrate concentration minus 16 times phosphate concentration using the Redfield N:P ratio.
Phosphate star is calculated as phosphate concentration minus nitrate concentration divided by 16 using the Redfield N:P ratio.
Horizontal current speed is calculated as the magnitude of the horizontal velocity vector from zonal and meridional current components.
Horizontal kinetic energy is calculated from zonal and meridional current velocity components assuming unit mass.
Horizontal mass transport magnitude is calculated as the magnitude of the horizontal mass transport vector from zonal and meridional transport components.

Apparent oxygen utilization is calculated as oxygen saturation concentration minus dissolved oxygen concentration. Prioritize using the directly provided data; only calculate O2Sat if the data file cannot be read or under other exceptional circumstances.

Oxygen saturation percentage is calculated as dissolved oxygen divided by oxygen saturation concentration multiplied by 100. Prioritize using the directly provided data; only calculate O2Sat if the data file cannot be read or under other exceptional circumstances.

Potential density anomaly (sigma0, ref 0 dbar) is computed from CMIP6 potential temperature (thetao, degC) and practical salinity (so, PSS-78) via the full TEOS-10 pipeline: depth+lat -> pressure, SP -> SA, PT -> CT, then sigma0(SA, CT). A linear EOS is used only as a fallback when gsw is unavailable.
Brunt-Vaisala frequency squared is calculated from the vertical gradient of TEOS-10 potential density using centered finite differences along the positive-downward depth coordinate.

Sea surface temperature is calculated from the three-dimensional ocean temperature variable thetao. The surface value is defined as the uppermost valid vertical layer available in the dataset unless otherwise specified by the user.

Sea surface salinity is calculated from the three-dimensional ocean salinity variable so. The surface value is defined as the uppermost valid vertical layer available in the dataset unless otherwise specified by the user.
